paper_summary.summary: AutoTool, developed at Huazhong University of Science and Technology, introduces a graph-based framework that significantly reduces LLM inference costs for agent tool selection and parameter filling by exploiting predictable "tool usage inertia". It achieves 15-25% fewer LLM calls and 10-40% less token consumption across multiple benchmarks, while maintaining or improving task completion rates.

title: AutoTool: Efficient Tool Selection for Large Language Model Agents

abstract: Learning from experience is critical for building capable large language model (LLM) agents, yet prevailing self-evolving paradigms remain inefficient: agents learn in isolation, repeatedly rediscover similar behaviors from limited experience, resulting in redundant exploration and poor generalization. To address this problem, we propose SkillX, a fully automated framework for constructing a \textbf{plug-and-play skill knowledge base} that can be reused across agents and environments. SkillX operates through a fully automated pipeline built on three synergistic innovations: \textit{(i) Multi-Level Skills Design}, which distills raw trajectories into three-tiered hierarchy of strategic plans, functional skills, and atomic skills; \textit{(ii) Iterative Skills Refinement}, which automatically revises skills based on execution feedback to continuously improve library quality; and \textit{(iii) Exploratory Skills Expansion}, which proactively generates and validates novel skills to expand coverage beyond seed training data. Using a strong backbone agent (GLM-4.6), we automatically build a reusable skill library and evaluate its transferability on challenging long-horizon, user-interactive benchmarks, including AppWorld, BFCL-v3, and $\tau^2$-Bench. Experiments show that SkillKB consistently improves task success and execution efficiency when plugged into weaker base agents, highlighting the importance of structured, hierarchical experience representations for generalizable agent learning. Our code will be publicly available soon at this https URL.

keyInsights: A multi-level hierarchical skill design provides a more advantageous and effective experience representation than direct trajectory retrieval or workflows, boosting agent performance.
An iterative refinement process and experience-guided exploration strategy are crucial for continuously improving skill quality, coverage, and sample efficiency in complex environments.
Distilling knowledge from a strong backbone agent into a structured skill base can effectively transfer capabilities to weaker inference models, expanding their operational boundaries.
originalProblem: LLM agents learn inefficiently from isolated experiences, leading to repeated rediscovery of behaviors and poor generalization of learned knowledge, especially in complex, data-scarce environments.
Existing experience representations lack simultaneous transferability, efficient retrieval, and direct executability, hindering effective reuse across tasks.
Prior skill-based systems often demand extensive context management and progressive disclosure, which limits their robustness and practical application.
results: SkillX consistently boosted the performance of various base LLM agents, with weaker models like Qwen3-32B achieving approximately a 10% gain in task success rates.
The multi-level skill representation proved more effective than other experience representations, leading to superior performance and transferability across different base models.

The framework improved agent execution efficiency by reducing the number of execution steps and input tokens, and its experience-guided expansion generated more novel skills and performance gains compared to random exploration.
solution: SkillX, an automated framework, constructs a plug-and-play skill knowledge base to facilitate the reuse of learned behaviors across different agents and environments.
It employs a multi-level hierarchical skill design (Planning, Functional, Atomic) for concise, composable, and robust experience representation.
The framework includes an iterative process of skill extraction, refinement (merging and filtering), and an experience-guided exploratory expansion for continuous improvement and broader coverage.
paper_summary.summary: SkillX is an automated framework that builds a plug-and-play skill knowledge base for LLM agents using a hierarchical representation of planning, functional, and atomic skills. This framework from Zhejiang University and Ant Digital Technologies improved task success rates by approximately 10% for weaker models and enhanced execution efficiency by reducing execution steps and input tokens across complex benchmarks.

title: SkillX: Automatically Constructing Skill Knowledge Bases for Agents

abstract: Computer-Using Agents (CUAs) aim to autonomously operate computer systems to complete real-world tasks. However, existing agentic systems remain difficult to scale and lag behind human performance. A key limitation is the absence of reusable and structured skill abstractions that capture how humans interact with graphical user interfaces and how to leverage these skills. We introduce CUA-Skill, a computer-using agentic skill base that encodes human computer-use knowledge as skills coupled with parameterized execution and composition graphs. CUA-Skill is a large-scale library of carefully engineered skills spanning common Windows applications, serving as a practical infrastructure and tool substrate for scalable, reliable agent development. Built upon this skill base, we construct CUA-Skill Agent, an end-to-end computer-using agent that supports dynamic skill retrieval, argument instantiation, and memory-aware failure recovery. Our results demonstrate that CUA-Skill substantially improves execution success rates and robustness on challenging end-to-end agent benchmarks, establishing a strong foundation for future computer-using agent development. On WindowsAgentArena, CUA-Skill Agent achieves state-of-the-art 57.5% (best of three) successful rate while being significantly more efficient than prior and concurrent approaches. The project page is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Encoding human computer-use knowledge into structured, parameterized skills significantly enhances the scalability, reliability, and generalizability of computer-using agents in complex desktop environments.

A flexible execution graph that unifies GUI primitives and scripts, and incorporates UI predicates, enables skills to adapt to dynamic UI states and reduce brittle behavior, leading to more robust task completion.

Integrating a reflective memory and a retrieve-augmented LLM planner allows for dynamic skill retrieval, robust argument instantiation, and effective error recovery, consistently improving task success rates across different LLM backbones.
originalProblem: Existing Computer-Using Agents (CUAs) struggle with scalability and reliability in long-horizon tasks, often due to representing desktop interaction as flat sequences of low-level actions.
The absence of structured, reusable skill abstractions limits CUAs' ability to generalize and transfer learned interaction patterns across different applications and UI states.

Current script-based agent skill models are often ineffective in GUI-driven desktop environments, which typically have limited and inconsistent programmatic APIs, leading to a high engineering burden for tool integration.
results: The CUA-Skill library, comprising 452 atomic skills, demonstrated an average execution success rate of 76.4% in isolated task executions on virtual machines.

CUA-Skill's ability to compose skills into executable trajectories achieved substantially higher success rates, outperforming UltraCUA by 1.7x and OpenAI Operator by 3.6x in trajectory generation tasks.

The CUA-Skill Agent achieved a state-of-the-art 57.5% best-of-three success rate on the WindowsAgentArena benchmark, outperforming prior and concurrent approaches like AgentS3 (56.6%) and Operator (37.4%).
solution: Developed CUA-Skill, a framework that encodes human computer-use knowledge into reusable, parameterized skills (S), each defined by an application, user intent, argument pool, and a parameterized execution graph (G_e).
Introduced skill execution graphs (G_e) that unify GUI interaction primitives and executable script actions, with edges guarded by UI predicates to adapt to common UI variations.

Designed the CUA-Skill Agent, an end-to-end architecture incorporating a retrieve-augmented LLM planner for dynamic skill selection and configuration, and a memory module for reflective execution and failure recovery.
paper_summary.summary: Microsoft researchers developed CUA-Skill, a framework that encodes human computer-use knowledge into reusable, parameterized skills for desktop environments. The accompanying CUA-Skill Agent achieves a 57.5% best-of-three success rate on the WindowsAgentArena benchmark, establishing state-of-the-art performance for computer-using agents.

title: CUA-Skill: Develop Skills for Computer Using Agent

abstract:
Large Language Model (LLM)-based search agents have shown remarkable
capabilities in solving complex tasks by dynamically decomposing problems and
addressing them through interleaved reasoning and retrieval. However, this
interleaved paradigm introduces substantial efficiency bottlenecks. First, we
observe that both highly accurate and overly approximate retrieval methods
degrade system efficiency: exact search incurs significant retrieval overhead,
while coarse retrieval requires additional reasoning steps during generation.
Second, we identify inefficiencies in system design, including improper
scheduling and frequent retrieval stalls, which lead to cascading latency --
where even minor delays in retrieval amplify end-to-end inference time. To
address these challenges, we introduce SearchAgent-X, a high-efficiency
inference framework for LLM-based search agents. SearchAgent-X leverages
high-recall approximate retrieval and incorporates two key techniques:
priority-aware scheduling and non-stall retrieval. Extensive experiments
demonstrate that SearchAgent-X consistently outperforms state-of-the-art
systems such as vLLM and HNSW-based retrieval across diverse tasks, achieving
up to 3.4$\times$ higher throughput and 5$\times$ lower latency, without
compromising generation quality. SearchAgent-X is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: The dynamic, multi-turn nature of LLM-based search agents creates unique efficiency bottlenecks related to retrieval latency and resource utilization.
There is an optimal intermediate accuracy level for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search that balances retrieval effectiveness and system throughput.
Even minor retrieval delays significantly impact overall system efficiency in search agents due to issues like improper scheduling and retrieval stalls.
System-level optimizations, such as priority scheduling and non-stall retrieval, can synergistically improve KV-cache utilization and overall performance.
originalProblem: LLM-based search agents, while enabling complex, multi-hop reasoning, face substantial computational overhead and latency challenges due to their dynamic, iterative nature.
They exhibit unique efficiency bottlenecks, including a non-monotonic relationship between retrieval accuracy and overall performance, and high sensitivity to retrieval latency.
Inefficiencies arise from improper scheduling, synchronous retrieval operations causing stalls, and poor KV-cache utilization.
results: SearchAgent-X achieved throughput improvements of 1.9x to 3.4x and latency reductions of 2x to 5x.
The framework maintained generation quality without degradation while significantly improving efficiency.
KV-cache utilization increased from 20% to over 80% through priority-aware scheduling.
The system exhibited superior scalability and consistent performance advantages at higher request rates.
solution: Introduces SearchAgent-X, an inference framework coordinating reasoning and retrieval to optimize search agent performance.
Employs High-Recall Approximate Retrieval to balance accuracy and efficiency, achieving 98% recall with speedup over exact search.
Implements Priority-Aware Scheduling based on retrieval count, context length, and waiting time to improve KV-cache utilization.
Utilizes Non-Stall Retrieval with adaptive termination to prevent LLM computation blocking and ensure efficient resource use.
paper_summary.summary: SearchAgent-X introduces a specialized inference framework designed to optimize LLM-based search agents, achieving up to 3.4x throughput improvements and 5x latency reductions without compromising generation quality. This is achieved through system-level optimizations that coordinate reasoning and retrieval operations.

title: Demystifying and Enhancing the Efficiency of Large Language Model Based  Search Agents

abstract:
LLM agents increasingly adopt skills as a reusable unit of composition. While skills are shared across diverse agent platforms, current systems treat them as raw context, causing the same skill to behave inconsistently for different agents. This fragility undermines skill portability and execution efficiency.
To address this challenge, we analyze 118,000 skills and draw inspiration from traditional compiler design. We treat skills as code and LLMs as heterogeneous processors. To make portability actionable, we decompose a skill's requirements into a set of primitive capabilities, and measure how well each model-harness pair supports them. Based on these capability profiles, we propose SkVM, a compilation and runtime system designed for portable and efficient skill execution. At compile time, SkVM performs capability-based compilation, environment binding, and concurrency extraction. At runtime, SkVM applies JIT code solidification and adaptive recompilation for performance optimization.
We evaluate SkVM across eight LLMs of varying scales and three agent harnesses, covering SkillsBench and representative skill tasks. Results demonstrate that SkVM significantly improves task completion rates across different models and environments while reducing token consumption by up to 40%. In terms of performance, SkVM achieves up to 3.2x speedup with enhanced parallelism, and 19-50x latency reduction through code solidification.

keyInsights: Applying classical compiler design principles (AOT/JIT compilation, capability modeling) to LLM agent 'skills' can systematically address challenges of portability, efficiency, and reliability in a heterogeneous LLM ecosystem.

Skills, when treated as a form of 'code' to be compiled for 'heterogeneous processors' (LLMs), can be transformed to adapt to varying model proficiencies, harness functionalities, and external environment dependencies.

A staged optimization approach, combining install-time AOT compilation with runtime JIT feedback and code solidification, is effective for both proactively adapting skills and reactively improving performance and correctness during execution.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent systems treat skills as raw contextual information, leading to inconsistent behavior, fragility, and portability issues across heterogeneous LLMs and agent harnesses.

Skills suffer from 'model mismatch' (LLMs vary in understanding skill instructions), 'harness mismatch' (agent harnesses differ in toolsets and interaction patterns), and 'environment mismatch' (missing external dependencies like packages or tools).
Current skill usage incurs substantial token overhead and increased latency, even when tasks are completed, and often degrades performance for less capable models.
results: SkVM increased task completion rates by an average of 15.3% across various LLMs and agent harnesses, with weaker models benefiting most substantially, and reduced task completion regressions from 15% to 4.5%.
The system achieved token savings of up to 40% for stronger models, primarily by avoiding error paths and redundant interactions, while also improving task success for most configurations.

Concurrency extraction yielded up to 3.2x end-to-end speedup, and code solidification resulted in significant efficiency gains, reducing execution time by 19-50x for PDF-extract tasks and 5x for API-bound tasks.
solution: SkVM employs an Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compiler that analyzes raw skill text against target model, harness, and host environment profiles, producing optimized skill variants through capability-based transformations, environment binding, and concurrency extraction.

The AOT compiler measures the gap between skill capability requirements (using 26 primitive capabilities with proficiency levels) and target competencies, applying compensation or substitution transforms to bridge gaps.

A runtime system manages compiled skill execution, incorporating Just-In-Time (JIT) optimizations like adaptive recompilation based on execution feedback, code solidification for parameterized code fragments to bypass LLM inference, and resource-aware parallel scheduling.
paper_summary.summary: SkVM introduces a compilation and runtime system for managing 'skills' used by Large Language Model agents, transforming natural language skill descriptions into optimized variants tailored for specific LLMs and execution environments. It improves task completion rates by 15.3% on average, reduces token consumption by up to 40%, and achieves up to 50x speedup through code solidification and 3.2x speedup via concurrency extraction across diverse models and agent harnesses.

title: SkVM: Compiling Skills for Efficient Execution Everywhere

abstract: Long-context capabilities are essential for large language models (LLMs) to tackle complex and long-input tasks. Despite numerous efforts made to optimize LLMs for long contexts, challenges persist in robustly processing long inputs. In this paper, we introduce GraphReader, a graph-based agent system designed to handle long texts by structuring them into a graph and employing an agent to explore this graph autonomously. Upon receiving a question, the agent first undertakes a step-by-step analysis and devises a rational plan. It then invokes a set of predefined functions to read node content and neighbors, facilitating a coarse-to-fine exploration of the graph. Throughout the exploration, the agent continuously records new insights and reflects on current circumstances to optimize the process until it has gathered sufficient information to generate an answer. Experimental results on the LV-Eval dataset reveal that GraphReader, using a 4k context window, consistently outperforms GPT-4-128k across context lengths from 16k to 256k by a large margin. Additionally, our approach demonstrates superior performance on four challenging single-hop and multi-hop benchmarks.

keyInsights: Structuring long text into a knowledge graph explicitly models multi-hop and long-range dependencies, which traditional linear or hierarchical representations miss.

An intelligent LLM agent can effectively navigate a knowledge graph using a rational plan and coarse-to-fine exploration, enabling robust information retrieval and reasoning even with a small LLM context window.
Strategic planning, intelligent initial node selection, and systematic neighbor exploration by the LLM agent are critical for efficient and accurate performance on complex, long-context tasks.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are fundamentally limited by fixed context windows, restricting their ability to process and reason over very long documents.
LLMs exhibit a 'lost in the middle' phenomenon, where crucial information located deep within long contexts is often overlooked, even when the context window is theoretically large enough.

Existing solutions like expanding model context windows are computationally expensive, while simpler agent-based methods (e.g., RAG) often struggle with complex, multi-hop reasoning over long, distributed information.
results: GraphReader consistently outperforms GPT-4-128k (native context) and other RAG/agent baselines on extremely long contexts (16k to 256k tokens) on the LV-Eval dataset, showing up to a 75% relative performance gain over GPT-4-128k at 128k tokens.
It achieves the best performance across multi-hop (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, MuSiQue) and single-hop (NarrativeQA) benchmarks on the LongBench suite, closely matching the 'Golden' baseline.

Ablation studies validate the critical importance of the 'rational plan' (e.g., 6.2% F1 drop on HotpotQA without it) and intelligent node selection (average 18% performance drop with random selection) for the agent's effectiveness.
solution: A three-phase framework: Graph Construction, Graph Exploration, and Answer Reasoning.
Graph Construction transforms long documents into a knowledge graph where key elements are nodes connected by co-occurrence relationships, with associated atomic facts and original chunks.

An autonomous LLM agent (with a small 4k context window) performs Graph Exploration by formulating a rational plan, intelligently selecting initial nodes, and iteratively navigating the graph through coarse-to-fine exploration of atomic facts and original chunks to collect supporting evidence.
paper_summary.summary: GraphReader enhances Large Language Models' ability to process extremely long documents by converting them into navigable knowledge graphs, enabling an intelligent LLM agent to systematically explore and reason over information. This approach significantly outperforms direct LLM context window expansion and existing agent-based methods on multi-hop QA benchmarks, showing up to a 75% relative performance gain over GPT-4-128k at 128k context length.

title: GraphReader: Building Graph-based Agent to Enhance Long-Context Abilities of Large Language Models

abstract: AI agents can extend their capabilities at inference time by loading reusable skills into context, yet equipping an agent with too many skills, particularly irrelevant ones, degrades performance. As community-driven skill repositories grow, agents need a way to selectively retrieve only the most relevant skills from a large library. We present SkillFlow, the first multi-stage retrieval pipeline designed for agent skill discovery, framing skill acquisition as an information retrieval problem over a corpus of ~36K community-contributed this http URL definitions indexed from GitHub. The pipeline progressively narrows a large candidate set through four stages: dense retrieval, two rounds of cross-encoder reranking, and LLM-based selection, balancing recall and precision at each stage. We evaluate SkillFlow on two coding benchmarks: SkillsBench, a benchmark of 87 tasks and 229 matched skills; and Terminal-Bench, a benchmark that provides only 89 tasks, and no matched skills. On SkillsBench, SkillFlow-retrieved skills raise Pass@1 from 9.2% to 16.4% (+78.3%, $p_{\text{adj}} = 3.64 \times 10^{-2}$), reaching 84.1% of the oracle ceiling, while on Terminal-Bench, agents readily use the retrieved skills (70.1% use rate) yet show no performance gain, revealing that retrieval alone is insufficient when the corpus lacks high-quality, executable skills for the target domain. SkillFlow demonstrates that framing skill acquisition as an information retrieval task is an effective strategy, and that the practical impact of skill-augmented agents hinges on corpus coverage and skill quality, particularly the density of runnable code and bundled artifacts.

organization_info.name: USDA/NSF AI Institute for Next Generation Food Systems

title: SkillFlow: Scalable and Efficient Agent Skill Retrieval System

abstract: Agent Skill framework, now widely and officially supported by major players such as GitHub Copilot, LangChain, and OpenAI, performs especially well with proprietary models by improving context engineering, reducing hallucinations, and boosting task accuracy. Based on these observations, an investigation is conducted to determine whether the Agent Skill paradigm provides similar benefits to small language models (SLMs). This question matters in industrial scenarios where continuous reliance on public APIs is infeasible due to data-security and budget constraints requirements, and where SLMs often show limited generalization in highly customized scenarios. This work introduces a formal mathematical definition of the Agent Skill process, followed by a systematic evaluation of language models of varying sizes across multiple use cases. The evaluation encompasses two open-source tasks and a real-world insurance claims data set. The results show that tiny models struggle with reliable skill selection, while moderately sized SLMs (approximately 12B - 30B) parameters) benefit substantially from the Agent Skill approach. Moreover, code-specialized variants at around 80B parameters achieve performance comparable to closed-source baselines while improving GPU efficiency. Collectively, these findings provide a comprehensive and nuanced characterization of the capabilities and constraints of the framework, while providing actionable insights for the effective deployment of Agent Skills in SLM-centered environments.

keyInsights: The Agent Skill framework significantly enhances the performance of moderately sized SLMs (12B-30B parameters), particularly for complex domain-specific tasks, by improving skill selection and execution correctness.
Extremely small SLMs (below 4B parameters) exhibit severe limitations in reliable skill selection and routing, indicating a minimum model scale is necessary for effective agentic deployment.

Code-specialized SLMs demonstrate superior accuracy and significantly reduced GPU VRAM-time consumption compared to instruction-tuned or 'thinking' variants of similar size, offering a more efficient option for industrial applications.
originalProblem: Industrial environments face significant constraints (GPU resources, latency, data security, privacy, budget) when deploying large, proprietary language models (LLMs) for agentic AI applications.
Small Language Models (SLMs) often exhibit limited generalization capabilities and struggle with complex skill routing, hindering their practical use in customized industrial scenarios.
The Agent Skill framework, while effective for large LLMs, had not been systematically evaluated for its benefits and applicability to SLMs, especially under real-world industrial constraints.
results: Most SLMs, especially those from 12B to 30B parameters, showed clear performance improvements with Agent Skills, largely maintaining high skill-selection accuracy across tasks.
On the challenging FiNER dataset, the Qwen3-80B-Instruct model's F1 score improved from 0.172 (Direct Instruction) to 0.647 (Agent Skill Instruction), demonstrating substantial gains.

Code-oriented models like Qwen3-80B-Coder achieved a higher Cls F1 of 0.658 on InsurBench under Agent Skill Instruction, while simultaneously providing enhanced VRAM efficiency, making them a competitive and cost-effective choice.
solution: The research formally defined the Agent Skill process as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) to provide a rigorous theoretical framework for agent decision-making and information acquisition.

A systematic evaluation was conducted across a wide range of open-source SLMs (270M to 80B parameters) using various context engineering strategies (Direct, Full-Skill, Agent Skill Instruction) on open-source (IMDB, FiNER) and proprietary industrial datasets (InsurBench).
Performance was measured using standard accuracy and F1 scores, complemented by a novel "Average VRAM Time (GB·min)" metric to quantify operational costs and efficiency in GPU-centric environments.
paper_summary.summary: This research systematically investigates the applicability of the Agent Skill framework to Small Language Models (SLMs) for industrial environments, demonstrating that moderately sized SLMs (12B-30B) and code-specialized 80B models achieve substantial performance gains and VRAM efficiency. The findings validate open-source SLMs as a viable and secure alternative to proprietary LLMs for complex, sensitive tasks by enhancing their generalization and task execution capabilities.

title: Agent Skill Framework: Perspectives on the Potential of Small Language Models in Industrial Environments

abstract: Improving GPU kernel efficiency is crucial for advancing AI systems. Recent work has explored leveraging large language models (LLMs) for GPU kernel generation and optimization. However, existing LLM-based kernel optimization pipelines typically rely on opaque, implicitly learned heuristics within the LLMs to determine optimization strategies. This leads to inefficient trial-and-error and weakly interpretable optimizations. Our key insight is to replace implicit heuristics with expert optimization skills that are knowledge-driven and aware of task trajectories. Specifically, we present KernelSkill, a multi-agent framework with a dual-level memory architecture. KernelSkill operates by coordinating agents with long-term memory of reusable expert skills and short-term memory to prevent repetitive backtracking. On KernelBench Levels 1-3, KernelSkill achieves a 100% success rate and average speedups of 5.44x, 2.82x, and 1.92x over Torch Eager on Levels 1, 2, and 3, respectively, outperforming prior baselines. Code is available at this https URL.
title: KernelSkill: A Multi-Agent Framework for GPU Kernel Optimization

abstract:
Recent developments in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for selecting
relevant tools from a tool knowledge base enable LLM agents to scale their
complex tool calling capabilities to hundreds or thousands of external tools,
APIs, or agents-as-tools. However, traditional RAG-based tool retrieval fails
to capture structured dependencies between tools, limiting the retrieval
accuracy of a retrieved tool's dependencies. For example, among a vector
database of tools, a "get stock price" API requires a "stock ticker" parameter
from a "get stock ticker" API, and both depend on OS-level internet
connectivity tools. In this paper, we address this limitation by introducing
Graph RAG-Tool Fusion, a novel plug-and-play approach that combines the
strengths of vector-based retrieval with efficient graph traversal to capture
all relevant tools (nodes) along with any nested dependencies (edges) within
the predefined tool knowledge graph. We also present ToolLinkOS, a new tool
selection benchmark of 573 fictional tools, spanning over 15 industries, each
with an average of 6.3 tool dependencies. We demonstrate that Graph RAG-Tool
Fusion achieves absolute improvements of 71.7% and 22.1% over na\"ive RAG on
ToolLinkOS and ToolSandbox benchmarks, respectively (mAP@10). ToolLinkOS
dataset is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Combining vector-based retrieval for semantic relevance with knowledge graph traversal for structured dependencies dramatically improves tool selection accuracy for LLM agents, particularly with large, interconnected toolsets.

Applying reranking to the initial top-k tools retrieved by vector search further enhances Graph RAG-Tool Fusion's performance, indicating that the quality of the initial semantic selection is critical.
The primary error source for Graph RAG-Tool Fusion is often the initial vector search failing to retrieve the correct primary tool, highlighting a key area for future improvement.
originalProblem: LLM agents struggle to scale their tool-calling capabilities to hundreds or thousands of external tools due to inefficient selection mechanisms.
Traditional vector-based RAG for tool selection fails to effectively capture and utilize structured dependencies between tools (e.g., one tool requiring parameters from another).
Existing benchmarks for tool selection do not adequately assess performance in scenarios involving complex, multi-hop inter-tool dependencies.
results: Graph RAG-Tool Fusion achieved an mAP@10 of 0.856 on the new ToolLinkOS dataset, an absolute improvement of 71.7% over Naïve RAG (0.210).
Adding a reranking step further boosted mAP@10 on ToolLinkOS to 0.927, an absolute improvement of 7% over the non-reranked Graph RAG-Tool Fusion.
The introduced ToolLinkOS dataset provides a new benchmark specifically designed to evaluate tool selection in scenarios with complex, dependency-rich tool ecosystems.
solution: PwC developed Graph RAG-Tool Fusion, a "plug-and-play" approach that integrates vector-based retrieval with knowledge graph traversal to identify both semantically relevant tools and their necessary direct and indirect dependencies.
Tools and their relationships are indexed within a Neo4j knowledge graph using specific node types (Core Tool, Regular Tool) and four predefined edge types capturing various dependencies.

The retrieval algorithm performs an initial vector search, followed by a Depth-First Search (DFS) traversal on the knowledge graph for each initially retrieved tool, aggregating and ordering all found tools for the LLM agent.
paper_summary.summary: PwC's Graph RAG-Tool Fusion enhances LLM agent tool selection by combining vector retrieval with knowledge graph traversal to accurately identify tools and their complex dependencies. This method showed a 71.7% absolute improvement in mAP@10 over traditional RAG on a new dependency-rich benchmark called ToolLinkOS.

title: Graph RAG-Tool Fusion

abstract: The rapid development of large language model (LLM)-based agents has unlocked new possibilities for autonomous multi-turn reasoning and tool-augmented decision-making. However, their real-world deployment is hindered by severe inefficiencies that arise not from isolated model inference, but from the systemic latency accumulated across reasoning loops, context growth, and heterogeneous tool interactions. This paper presents AgentInfer, a unified framework for end-to-end agent acceleration that bridges inference optimization and architectural design. We decompose the problem into four synergistic components: AgentCollab, a hierarchical dual-model reasoning framework that balances large- and small-model usage through dynamic role assignment; AgentSched, a cache-aware hybrid scheduler that minimizes latency under heterogeneous request patterns; AgentSAM, a suffix-automaton-based speculative decoding method that reuses multi-session semantic memory to achieve low-overhead inference acceleration; and AgentCompress, a semantic compression mechanism that asynchronously distills and reorganizes agent memory without disrupting ongoing reasoning. Together, these modules form a Self-Evolution Engine capable of sustaining efficiency and cognitive stability throughout long-horizon reasoning tasks. Experiments on the BrowseComp-zh and DeepDiver benchmarks demonstrate that through the synergistic collaboration of these methods, AgentInfer reduces ineffective token consumption by over 50%, achieving an overall 1.8-2.5 times speedup with preserved accuracy. These results underscore that optimizing for agentic task completion-rather than merely per-token throughput-is the key to building scalable, efficient, and self-improving intelligent systems.

keyInsights: Optimizing LLM agents requires a holistic, multi-layered approach that bridges architectural design with low-level inference acceleration, as isolated optimizations can be insufficient or detrimental.

Asymmetric resource allocation and self-evaluation-driven adaptivity (e.g., using smaller models for routine tasks and larger models for complex problems) can significantly improve efficiency without sacrificing reasoning quality.

Intelligent, asynchronous context management and dynamic scheduling based on KV cache pressure are crucial for mitigating context explosion and optimizing resource utilization for heterogeneous agent workloads.
originalProblem: Autonomous LLM agents face severe inefficiencies in real-world deployment due to cumulative latency across multi-turn reasoning, context growth, and heterogeneous tool interactions.

Traditional LLM inference optimizations focus on per-token throughput (TPS), which is often insufficient or counterproductive for agents whose efficiency is measured by inter-step time-to-completion and reasoning stability.
Key bottlenecks include the high cost of continuous large model invocation, rapid context explosion, suboptimal scheduling for agentic workloads, and slow token generation for repeated patterns.
results: AgentInfer achieved a 1.8x-2.5x speedup in end-to-end task latency and reduced ineffective token consumption by over 50%, with preserved reasoning accuracy.
AgentCollab recovered most of the large model's accuracy (33.8% vs. 34.6%) while providing a 1.32x speedup compared to a large-model-only baseline.

AgentCompress effectively reduced context from over 40K to around 20K tokens and improved tool call efficiency, while AgentSched increased KV cache hit rates from 63% to 72% and reduced average LLM call latency by 9.8%. AgentSAM further reduced LLM latency by up to 26%.
solution: AgentInfer, a unified hierarchical framework, co-designs optimizations at both the reasoning process and inference engine levels.

It integrates four synergistic components: AgentCollab (multi-agent collaboration with self-evaluation), AgentCompress (semantic agent memory compression), AgentSched (adaptive KV-cache-aware scheduler), and AgentSAM (context-enhanced speculative decoding).
The framework shifts the optimization paradigm to holistic, task-centric metrics like End-to-End Task Latency and Total Computational Cost, focusing on agentic task completion.
paper_summary.summary: Huawei researchers developed AgentInfer, a unified framework to accelerate large language model (LLM)-based autonomous agents by co-designing inference architecture and system components. It achieves a 1.8x-2.5x speedup in end-to-end latency and reduces ineffective token consumption by over 50% while preserving reasoning accuracy on complex benchmarks.

title: Towards Efficient Agents: A Co-Design of Inference Architecture and System

abstract:
Code efficiency is a fundamental aspect of software quality, yet how to harness large language models (LLMs) to optimize programs remains challenging. Prior approaches have sought for one-shot rewriting, retrieved exemplars, or prompt-based search, but they do not explicitly distill reusable optimization knowledge, which limits generalization beyond individual instances.
In this paper, we present EffiSkill, a framework for code-efficiency optimization that builds a portable optimization toolbox for LLM-based agents. The key idea is to model recurring slow-to-fast transformations as reusable agent skills that capture both concrete transformation mechanisms and higher-level optimization strategies. EffiSkill adopts a two-stage design: Stage I mines Operator and Meta Skills from large-scale slow/fast program pairs to build a skill library; Stage II applies this library to unseen programs through execution-free diagnosis, skill retrieval, plan composition, and candidate generation, without runtime feedback.
Results on EffiBench-X show that EffiSkill achieves higher optimization success rates, improving over the strongest baseline by 3.69 to 12.52 percentage points across model and language settings. These findings suggest that mechanism-level skill reuse provides a useful foundation for execution-free code optimization, and that the resulting skill library can serve as a reusable resource for broader agent workflows.

keyInsights: Explicitly distilling recurring transformation patterns into a structured, portable library of "agent skills" allows LLMs to apply abstract optimization principles rather than merely imitating surface-level code patterns.
A skill-guided diagnosis, retrieval, and plan composition pipeline can effectively enable execution-free code optimization, addressing practical limitations of online feedback.
Diversified plan exploration and the accurate retrieval of relevant skills are crucial components for discovering high-quality, functionally correct, and measurably faster code.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based code optimization methods often operate at an instance-specific level, struggling with multi-step reasoning and failing to explicitly distill reusable optimization knowledge.
Prior data-driven approaches frequently rely on iterative execution feedback during the optimization process, which is often unavailable or costly in real-world software engineering scenarios.
Traditional code optimization relies on manually designed rules and expert-driven techniques, which typically offer limited coverage and require substantial human effort.
results: EffiSkill consistently achieved higher optimization success rates, outperforming the strongest baselines by up to +12.52 percentage points for OPT@8 on EffiBench-X across Python and C++ using GPT-5-mini and Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct.

Ablation studies confirmed that each component of EffiSkill's inference stage (skill retrieval, relevant skill selection, and multi-plan composition) significantly contributed to its overall performance.

The learned skill library consists of 29 compact and diverse Operator Skills, categorized into five semantic families, demonstrating a core set of frequently applicable skills that enable the composition of effective optimization "recipes."
solution: EffiSkill employs a two-stage design: offline skill mining, where recurrent slow-to-fast program transformations are distilled into structured "agent skills," and skill-guided optimization during inference.

It formalizes two types of agent skills: "Operator Skills" that encode concrete transformation mechanisms (e.g., algorithm replacement), and "Meta Skills" that capture higher-level control logic for orchestrating optimization.

During inference, the framework utilizes Meta Skills for diagnosis, retrieves relevant Operator Skills, composes diverse optimization plans, and generates optimized code candidates without execution feedback.
paper_summary.summary: EffiSkill, a framework developed by researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, University of California, Berkeley, and other institutions, enables Large Language Models to optimize code efficiency by distilling and reusing optimization knowledge as agent skills. It achieved up to +12.52 percentage points higher OPT@8 success rates compared to state-of-the-art baselines on the EffiBench-X benchmark for Python and C++, enabling execution-free code improvement.

title: EffiSkill: Agent Skill Based Automated Code Efficiency Optimization

abstract: As agent systems powered by large language models (LLMs) advance, improving performance in context understanding, tool usage, and long-horizon execution has become critical. However, existing agent frameworks and benchmarks provide limited visibility into execution-level behavior, making failures in tool invocation, state tracking, and context management difficult to diagnose. This paper presents Jenius-Agent, a system-level agent framework grounded in real-world deployment experience. It integrates adaptive prompt generation, context-aware tool orchestration, and layered memory mechanism to stabilize execution and improve robustness in long-horizon, tool-augmented tasks. Beyond system design, we introduce an evaluation methodology that jointly measures procedural fidelity, semantic correctness, and efficiency. This framework makes agent behavior observable as a structured execution process and enables systematic analysis of failure modes not captured by output-only metrics. Experiments on Jenius-bench show substantial improvements in task completion rate, with up to a 35 percent relative gain over the base agent, along with reduced token consumption, response latency, and tool invocation failures. The framework is already deployed in Jenius ({this https URL}), providing a lightweight and scalable solution for robust, protocol-compatible autonomous agents.
title: Jenius Agent: Towards Experience-Driven Accuracy Optimization in Real-World Scenarios

abstract:
Humans excel at reusing prior knowledge to address new challenges and
developing skills while solving problems. This paradigm becomes increasingly
popular in the development of autonomous agents, as it develops systems that
can self-evolve in response to new challenges like human beings. However,
previous methods suffer from limited training efficiency when expanding new
skills and fail to fully leverage prior knowledge to facilitate new task
learning. In this paper, we propose Parametric Skill Expansion and Composition
(PSEC), a new framework designed to iteratively evolve the agents' capabilities
and efficiently address new challenges by maintaining a manageable skill
library. This library can progressively integrate skill primitives as
plug-and-play Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules in parameter-efficient
finetuning, facilitating efficient and flexible skill expansion. This structure
also enables the direct skill compositions in parameter space by merging LoRA
modules that encode different skills, leveraging shared information across
skills to effectively program new skills. Based on this, we propose a
context-aware module to dynamically activate different skills to
collaboratively handle new tasks. Empowering diverse applications including
multi-objective composition, dynamics shift, and continual policy shift, the
results on D4RL, DSRL benchmarks, and the DeepMind Control Suite show that PSEC
exhibits superior capacity to leverage prior knowledge to efficiently tackle
new challenges, as well as expand its skill libraries to evolve the
capabilities. Project website: this https URL

keyInsights: Composing skills directly in the neural network's parameter space (early fusion) is more effective than combining them at the action or noise level.
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules provide an efficient and isolated way to represent individual skills, enabling their direct interpolation and composition.
Dynamic, context-aware blending of skills based on the current state is essential for agents to adapt to complex and multi-objective tasks.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents typically learn each new task from scratch, leading to high sample and computational inefficiency.
Existing methods struggle with catastrophic forgetting when agents continually acquire new skills.
Prior skill composition approaches often use simplistic skill representations or fixed combination weights, limiting expressiveness and adaptability to dynamic environments.
results: Achieved superior performance in multi-objective composition benchmarks (DSRL), effectively balancing conflicting objectives like reward and safety, outperforming state-of-the-art safe offline RL methods.

Demonstrated high sample and training efficiency in continual policy learning, acquiring new skills with significantly less data (e.g., 10 trajectories) and showing improved performance as the skill library grows.
Enabled robust transfer learning and rapid adaptation to dynamics shifts with limited data, surpassing various offline RL and sample-efficient baselines.
solution: Models policies using expressive diffusion models for diverse behavior representation.
Employs Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to efficiently encode each new skill as a low-rank module, mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
Introduces a context-aware modular network that dynamically blends these LoRA modules directly in the policy's parameter space based on the current state.
paper_summary.summary: PSEC is a framework that allows autonomous agents to efficiently acquire new skills and compose existing ones by representing policies with diffusion models, encoding skills as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules, and dynamically blending these modules in parameter space via a context-aware network. This method demonstrates superior performance and sample efficiency across tasks involving multi-objective control, continual policy learning, and adaptation to dynamics shifts compared to various baselines.

title: Skill Expansion and Composition in Parameter Space

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to sequential decision-making through in-context learning (ICL), yet their effectiveness is highly sensitive to prompt quality. Effective prompts should meet three principles: focus on decision-critical information, provide step-level granularity, and minimize reliance on expert annotations through label efficiency. However, existing ICL methods often fail to satisfy all three criteria simultaneously. Motivated by these challenges, we introduce SkillGen, a skill-based ICL framework for structured sequential reasoning. It constructs an action-centric, domain-level graph from sampled trajectories, identifies high-utility actions via temporal-difference credit assignment, and retrieves step-wise skills to generate fine-grained, context-aware prompts. We further present a theoretical analysis showing that focusing on high-utility segments supports task identifiability and informs more effective ICL prompt design. Experiments on ALFWorld, BabyAI, and ScienceWorld, using both open-source and proprietary LLMs, show that SkillGen achieves consistent gains, improving progress rate by 5.9%-16.5% on average across models.

keyInsights: Focused prompts, containing high-utility content and minimal irrelevant information, enhance task identifiability for LLMs, as supported by theoretical analysis.

Temporal-Difference learning can effectively assign dense credit to individual actions within a domain graph, even with sparse progress signals, which is vital for extracting meaningful, reusable skills.
Decomposing domain knowledge into concise 'golden segments' and granular 'step-wise skills' provides a powerful and label-efficient mechanism for guiding LLM agents in sequential decision-making.
originalProblem: Large Language Models acting as agents in sequential decision-making are highly sensitive to the quality and design of in-context learning prompts.
Current in-context learning methods for multi-step tasks often fail to provide focused, granular, step-level guidance while maintaining label efficiency.
Existing approaches struggle with prompt redundancy, lack of fine-grained, context-aware instructions, or heavy reliance on expensive expert demonstrations.
results: SkillGen consistently surpassed existing baselines, including 0-shot, 1-shot, Leap, Synapse, and Trad, in Progress Rate, Success Rate, and Area Under the Progress Curve across ALFWorld, BabyAI, and ScienceWorld.
For instance, on ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, it achieved 68.0% Progress Rate and 55.2% Success Rate, significantly outperforming Synapse's 44.8% PR and 19.4% SR.

The framework also demonstrated superior token efficiency, yielding greater task progress per 1,000 tokens compared to baselines, and its core components (golden segments, step-wise skills, TD-based credit assignment) were validated through ablation studies.
solution: SkillGen develops a three-stage framework that first constructs a domain knowledge graph from sampled trajectories, then extracts high-utility 'golden segments' and local 'step-wise skills'.
It employs Temporal-Difference (TD) learning on the domain graph to assign dense credit to actions, enabling the identification of truly decision-critical skills from sparse progress signals.

During online inference, a frozen LLM generates actions using a composite prompt that integrates the global golden segment, contextually retrieved step-wise skills, and the current trajectory history.
paper_summary.summary: SkillGen introduces a three-stage framework that extracts domain-oriented, action-centric skills and high-utility segments to enhance in-context learning for Large Language Model agents in sequential decision-making. The approach demonstrated superior performance and token efficiency across ALFWorld, BabyAI, and ScienceWorld benchmarks, outperforming existing baselines.

title: SkillGen: Learning Domain Skills for In-Context Sequential Decision Making

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are moving beyond static uses and are now powering agents that learn continually during their interaction with external environments. For example, agents can learn reusable skills while navigating web pages or toggling new tools. However, existing methods for skill learning often create skills that are over-specialized to a single website and fail to generalize. We introduce PolySkill, a new framework that enables agents to learn generalizable and compositional skills. The core idea, inspired by polymorphism in software engineering, is to decouple a skill's abstract goal (what it accomplishes) and its concrete implementation (how it is executed). Experiments show that our method (1) improves skill reuse by 1.7x on seen websites and (2) boosts success rates by up to 9.4% on Mind2Web and 13.9% on unseen websites, while reducing steps by over 20%. (3) In self-exploration settings without specified tasks, our framework improves the quality of proposed tasks and enables agents to learn generalizable skills that work across different sites. By enabling the agent to identify and refine its own goals, the PolySkill enhances the agent's ability to learn a better curriculum, leading to the acquisition of more generalizable skills compared to baseline methods. This work provides a practical path toward building agents capable of continual learning in adaptive environments. Our findings show that separating a skill's goal from its execution is a crucial step toward developing autonomous agents that can learn and generalize across the open web continuously. Our code can be found in this https URL.

keyInsights: Polymorphic abstraction enables the creation of generalizable skills that are reusable across diverse web environments, moving beyond over-specialized approaches.
Structuring skill learning through a hierarchical, abstract-to-concrete approach accelerates skill acquisition on unseen websites by providing focused exploration goals.
Effective skill induction, which emphasizes reusability and compositionality, directly correlates with improved task efficiency (fewer steps) and overall task success rates.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based web agents acquire skills that are often over-specialized, failing to generalize across diverse websites or even varied tasks within the same functional domain.
Current skill learning methods produce skills tied to specific UI elements, hindering adaptation to new web environments and limiting effective continual learning.
Evaluation metrics primarily focus on final task success, failing to adequately diagnose skill transfer failures or quantify the reusability and compositional value of learned skills.
results: PolySkill consistently outperformed baselines like ASI across Mind2Web and WebArena benchmarks, achieving up to a 9.4% higher success rate on Mind2Web and a 4.9% overall performance advantage over ASI on WebArena in continual learning scenarios.

The framework demonstrated significantly improved skill generalizability, with 73% of learned skills transferring to unseen websites, a substantial increase from less than 31% for prior methods, and reduced operational steps by over 20%.

PolySkill effectively mitigated catastrophic forgetting in continual learning settings and enabled autonomous skill acquisition in task-free explorative environments, achieving 43.1% success on held-out shopping benchmarks and 66.2% on GitLab, and 84% on GitHub.
solution: Introduces the PolySkill framework, which leverages polymorphic abstraction from software engineering to decouple a skill's abstract goal from its site-specific implementation.

Establishes a domain-driven skill hierarchy with abstract classes defining high-level goals and method signatures, and concrete subclasses providing distinct, website-specific implementations for these methods.

Guides the LLM-based agent to first induce abstract class interfaces for a new domain and then define concrete skill implementations within site-specific classes that inherit from these abstract blueprints.
paper_summary.summary: PolySkill is a framework that enhances the generalizability of skills for LLM-based web agents by decoupling abstract goals from concrete, site-specific implementations. The framework achieves up to a 9.4% increase in task success rate on Mind2Web benchmarks and improves skill transfer to unseen websites by 73% compared to previous methods, while reducing operational steps by over 20%.

title: PolySkill: Learning Generalizable Skills Through Polymorphic Abstraction

abstract:
Generative skill acquisition enables embodied agents to actively learn a
scalable and evolving repertoire of control skills, crucial for the advancement
of large decision models. While prior approaches often rely on supervision
signals from generalist agents (e.g., LLMs), their effectiveness in complex 3D
environments remains unclear; exhaustive evaluation incurs substantial
computational costs, significantly hindering the efficiency of skill learning.
Inspired by recent successes in verification models for mathematical reasoning,
we propose VERGSA (Verifying Embodied Reasoning in Generative Skill
Acquisition), a framework that systematically integrates real-time verification
principles into embodied skill learning. VERGSA establishes 1) a seamless
extension from verification of mathematical reasoning into embodied learning by
dynamically incorporating contextually relevant tasks into prompts and defining
success metrics for both subtasks and overall tasks, and 2) an automated,
scalable reward labeling scheme that synthesizes dense reward signals by
iteratively finalizing the contribution of scene configuration and subtask
learning to overall skill acquisition. To the best of our knowledge, this
approach constitutes the first comprehensive training dataset for
verification-driven generative skill acquisition, eliminating arduous manual
reward engineering. Experiments validate the efficacy of our approach: 1) the
exemplar task pool improves the average task success rates by 21%, 2) our
verification model boosts success rates by 24% for novel tasks and 36% for
encountered tasks, and 3) outperforms LLM-as-a-Judge baselines in verification
quality.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

title: Real-Time Verification of Embodied Reasoning for Generative Skill  Acquisition

abstract: Agent skills, which are reusable, domain-specific knowledge artifacts, have become a popular mechanism for extending LLM-based agents, yet formally benchmarking skill usage performance remains scarce. Existing skill benchmarking efforts focus on overly idealized conditions, where LLMs are directly provided with hand-crafted, narrowly-tailored task-specific skills for each task, whereas in many realistic settings, the LLM agent may have to search for and select relevant skills on its own, and even the closest matching skills may not be well-tailored for the task. In this paper, we conduct the first comprehensive study of skill utility under progressively challenging realistic settings, where agents must retrieve skills from a large collection of 34k real-world skills and may not have access to any hand-curated skills. Our findings reveal that the benefits of skills are fragile: performance gains degrade consistently as settings become more realistic, with pass rates approaching no-skill baselines in the most challenging scenarios. To narrow this gap, we study skill refinement strategies, including query-specific and query-agnostic approaches, and we show that query-specific refinement substantially recovers lost performance when the initial skills are of reasonable relevance and quality. We further demonstrate the generality of retrieval and refinement on Terminal-Bench 2.0, where they improve the pass rate of Claude Opus 4.6 from 57.7% to 65.5%. Our results, consistent across multiple models, highlight both the promise and the current limitations of skills for LLM-based agents. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The benefits of agentic skills, while present in idealized scenarios, consistently degrade as evaluation settings become more realistic, with weaker models sometimes performing worse with irrelevant skills.

Key bottlenecks limiting skill utility include the agent's inability to reliably select relevant skills from a noisy pool, imperfect retrieval from large collections, and difficulty in adapting general skills to specific tasks.

Query-specific skill refinement significantly improves agent performance by enabling task-aware adaptation and synthesis of information from retrieved skills, recovering a substantial portion of lost utility.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent skill benchmarks often relied on idealized conditions, providing pre-selected and hand-crafted skills directly to agents.

Agents face challenges in real-world scenarios, including independently selecting relevant skills from many options, retrieving skills from large unorganized repositories, and adapting general-purpose skills to specific queries.
The true utility and limitations of agentic skills in practical, less-than-ideal circumstances remained largely unquantified.
results: Agentic search, which allows LLMs to iteratively formulate queries, significantly outperformed direct semantic search by 18.7 percentage points in Recall@3 for skill retrieval.
Pass rates for Claude Opus on SKILLSBENCH degraded from 55.4% (curated + forced load) to 38.4% (retrieved without curated skills), closely approaching the no-skill baseline of 35.4%.

Query-specific refinement improved Claude's pass rate on SKILLSBENCH (retrieved w/ curated) from 40.1% to 48.2% and demonstrated broader applicability by enhancing performance on TERMINAL-BENCH 2.0 from 61.4% to 65.5%.
solution: Assembled a large dataset of 34,198 real-world skills to simulate realistic retrieval and adaptation conditions for LLM agents.
Developed a skill search engine and evaluated various retrieval strategies (keyword, semantic, hybrid, agentic search) to identify effective skill discovery methods.
Assessed LLM agent performance across six progressively realistic evaluation settings on SKILLSBENCH and TERMINAL-BENCH 2.0, introducing challenges of selection, retrieval, and adaptation.
Investigated query-specific and query-agnostic skill refinement strategies to improve skill utility by making retrieved content more relevant and actionable.
paper_summary.summary: This study systematically benchmarks the efficacy of LLM agentic skills under progressively realistic conditions, incorporating challenges like skill selection, retrieval from large repositories, and adaptation. It reveals that skill benefits significantly diminish in such settings but can be substantially recovered through query-specific skill refinement strategies.

title: How Well Do Agentic Skills Work in the Wild: Benchmarking LLM Skill Usage in Realistic Settings

abstract: LLM-based coding agents rely on \emph{skills}, pre-packaged instruction sets that extend agent capabilities, yet every token of skill content injected into the context window incurs both monetary cost and attention dilution. To understand the severity of this problem, we conduct a large-scale empirical study of 55,315 publicly available skills and find systemic inefficiencies: 26.4\% lack routing descriptions entirely, over 60\% of body content is non-actionable, and reference files can inject tens of thousands of tokens per invocation. Motivated by these findings, we present \textsc{SkillReducer}, a two-stage optimization framework. Stage~1 optimizes the routing layer by compressing verbose descriptions and generating missing ones via adversarial delta debugging. Stage~2 restructures skill bodies through taxonomy-driven classification and progressive disclosure, separating actionable core rules from supplementary content loaded on demand, validated by faithfulness checks and a self-correcting feedback loop. Evaluated on 600 skills and the SkillsBench benchmark, \textsc{SkillReducer} achieves 48\% description compression and 39\% body compression while improving functional quality by 2.8\%, revealing a \emph{less-is-more} effect where removing non-essential content reduces distraction in the context window. These benefits transfer across five models from four families with a mean retention of 0.965, and generalize to an independent agent framework.

keyInsights: LLM agent skills contain significant redundancy in both their descriptions and bodies, with over 60% of skill body content being non-actionable information that consumes tokens unnecessarily.

Structured, content-aware optimization, inspired by software engineering principles, is more effective for LLM agent skills than generic token-level compression, allowing for fine-grained reduction while preserving functionality.

Removing non-essential content from LLM agent skills not only reduces token costs but can also improve agent performance, demonstrating a "less-is-more" effect by reducing cognitive load or distraction within the context window.
originalProblem: LLM agent skills frequently suffer from "skill bloat," consuming excessive tokens due to verbose descriptions, non-actionable content, and inefficient handling of reference files.
High token costs resulting from inefficient skills lead to increased monetary expenses for LLM agent users and can cause attention dilution, potentially degrading agent performance.

Existing prompt compression techniques are typically token-level and do not adequately address the structured, semantic content of LLM agent skills, which interleave actionable rules, examples, and background information.
results: SkillReducer achieved significant token reductions: a mean of 48.0% for skill descriptions, 39.0% for skill bodies, and 26.8% in overall end-to-end input token savings.
The framework preserved functional quality with 100% routing preservation and an 86.0% task pass rate, with compressed skills outperforming original ones on average (0.742 vs. 0.722).

Optimization benefits generalized across five LLM models from four families (mean retention 0.965) and an independent agent framework (mean retention 0.944), confirming the robustness and transferability of the approach.
solution: SkillReducer employs a two-stage optimization framework, using LLM-driven delta debugging with a simulated oracle to minimize skill descriptions to their minimally sufficient routing-relevant clauses and generate descriptions when missing.

It restructures skill bodies by classifying content (e.g., core rules, examples, background) into a compact core module and on-demand reference modules, with cross-file deduplication for external references.

The framework integrates quality gates for faithfulness verification and task-based evaluation with a feedback loop, which can promote critical non-core items to ensure functional quality is preserved or restored.
paper_summary.summary: SkillReducer is a framework developed by researchers including those from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology to optimize Large Language Model (LLM) agent skills for token efficiency. It achieves substantial token reductions, such as a 48.0% decrease in description tokens and a 39.0% decrease in body tokens, while maintaining or improving agent functional quality across diverse LLMs and frameworks.

title: SkillReducer: Optimizing LLM Agent Skills for Token Efficiency

abstract: Graph Chain-of-Thought (Graph-CoT) enables large language models (LLMs) to perform step-by-step reasoning over graph-structured knowledge, but existing pipelines suffer from low accuracy, excessive token usage, high latency, and low throughput due to single-agent monolithic prompts, repeated context re-encoding, and inefficient serving execution. We present GLM, the first multi-agent Graph-CoT system co-designed with an optimized LLM serving architecture. GLM decomposes reasoning into specialized agents for classification, reasoning, action generation, and graph retrieval, enabling branching and selective context sharing to reduce prompt length and reasoning iterations while preserving reasoning quality, thereby improving accuracy and reducing overall token consumption. To scale inference, we introduce a Graph-CoT-aware LLM inference mechanism with graph-specific KV-cache management, priority-based eviction, and pipelined execution to improve serving efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that GLM improves answer accuracy by up to 38%, reduces token cost by up to 95.7%, lowers inference latency by 90.3%, and achieves up to 15.1x higher throughput compared to state-of-the-art Graph-CoT baselines, enabling efficient adoption for complex real-world reasoning at scale.

keyInsights: Decomposing complex graph reasoning into specialized multi-agent interactions with executable code significantly enhances accuracy, reduces token consumption, and improves reasoning stability by avoiding monolithic prompt issues.

Co-designing the reasoning framework with tailored LLM serving optimizations is crucial for achieving practical scalability and efficiency for graph-structured data, particularly concerning latency and throughput.

Optimized KV cache management, such as vertex-centric reuse and priority-based eviction, coupled with pipelined execution, can drastically improve LLM inference efficiency by maximizing cache hits and minimizing I/O latency.
originalProblem: Existing single-agent Graph Chain-of-Thought (Graph-CoT) frameworks incur substantial token overhead (over 40,000 tokens/query) and high costs (>$3/query) due to monolithic prompts, leading to unstable reasoning and low accuracy from the "lost-in-the-middle" effect.

Graph-CoT systems suffer from high end-to-end latencies (11-39 seconds/query) and poor throughput scalability, stemming from large LLM inference overheads, graph retrieval latency, and inefficient KV cache management.
results: GLM achieved up to 38% higher accuracy over Graph-CoT baselines and up to 60% over Base LLMs on a diverse graph reasoning benchmark (GRBench).
It reduced token consumption by up to 95.7% (averaging 1,538-2,974 tokens/instance) and end-to-end latency by up to 90.3% (2.8-5.9 seconds/query) compared to Graph-CoT.
The system delivered up to 15.1x higher throughput (6.8-9.1 queries per second) and saw individual optimizations like pipelined execution reducing latency by up to 47.8%.
solution: Developed a multi-agent Graph-CoT framework, GLM, comprising Classification, Reasoning, and Action Agents, to decompose complex tasks and generate executable Python code for precise graph information retrieval.

Introduced Graph-CoT-aware LLM inference optimizations, including a vertex-centric KV cache reuse model, a priority-based KV cache eviction policy, and a pipelined execution strategy to overlap retrieval and LLM decoding.
paper_summary.summary: GLM introduces a co-designed multi-agent reasoning framework and optimized LLM serving architecture to achieve scalable graph Chain-of-Thought reasoning. This system significantly improves accuracy and efficiency for complex graph-structured data, reducing token costs by up to 95.7% and inference latency by up to 90.3% compared to prior methods.

title: Scaling Graph Chain-of-Thought Reasoning: A Multi-Agent Framework with Efficient LLM Serving

abstract: General-purpose computer-use agents have shown impressive performance across diverse digital environments. However, our new benchmark, OSExpert-Eval, indicates they remain far less helpful than human experts. Although inference-time scaling enables adaptation, these agents complete complex tasks inefficiently with degraded performance, transfer poorly to unseen UIs, and struggle with fine-grained action sequences. To solve the problem, we introduce a GUI-based depth-first search (GUI-DFS) exploration algorithm to comprehensively explore and verify an environment's unit functions. The agent then exploits compositionality between unit skills to self-construct a curriculum for composite tasks. To support fine-grained actions, we curate a database of action primitives for agents to discover during exploration; these are saved as a skill set once the exploration is complete. We use the learned skills to improve the agent's performance and efficiency by (1) enriching agents with ready-to-use procedural knowledge, allowing them to plan only once for long trajectories and generate accurate actions, and (2) enabling them to end inference-time scaling earlier by realizing their boundary of capabilities. Extensive experiments show that our environment-learned agent takes a meaningful step toward expert-level computer use, achieving a around 20 percent performance gain on OSExpert-Eval and closing the efficiency gap to humans by around 80 percent

keyInsights: Autonomous, environment-driven exploration can effectively build a verifiable skill set for computer-use agents, reducing reliance on human-curated data and improving performance on complex tasks and UI generalization.

Explicitly documenting exploration failures as 'skill boundaries' and combining this with a fast planner significantly improves agent efficiency by preventing prolonged, unproductive attempts on infeasible tasks.

Integrating dedicated fine-grained action primitives with grounding-based verification offers a robust method for achieving precise control in diverse UIs, providing an alternative to extensive manual labeling for low-level actions.
originalProblem: General-purpose computer-use agents struggle with complex, long-horizon tasks, showing degraded performance and poor generalization to unseen or creatively designed user interfaces.

Existing agents have difficulties with fine-grained actions requiring precise control and are highly inefficient, often 5–50 times slower than human experts due to trial-and-error exploration and iterative planning.
Current methods rely heavily on human-annotated data, which is not scalable for continuously evolving real-world interfaces, leading to a lack of environment-specific procedural knowledge.
results: OSExpert increased success rates from near-zero for baselines to 20-30% on OSExpert-Eval's complex long-horizon, unseen UI generalization, and fine-grained action tasks.
The environment-learned agent reduced task completion time, closing the efficiency gap to human experts by approximately 80% compared to the most efficient existing agents.

Ablation studies demonstrated that environment-specific procedural knowledge from exploration drove performance improvements, while the skill boundary check was the primary source of efficiency gains by preventing prolonged, futile attempts.
solution: A GUI-DFS (Depth-First Search) exploration algorithm systematically discovers unit-level functionalities of a digital environment to build an expert-level, environment-specific skill set.

A fast planner (LoRA-tuned language model) generates entire task plans in a single pass, and a 'skill boundary check' mechanism identifies and stops infeasible tasks early to enhance execution efficiency.

A database of fine-grained action primitives, integrated with grounding-based verification (e.g., using SAM2), enables precise control for complex manipulations and consolidates successful fine-grained procedures into the agent's skill set.
paper_summary.summary: OSExpert presents an environment-learning framework that enables computer-use agents to autonomously acquire and compose professional skills through self-exploration. This approach improves task success rates on complex, open-ended tasks in professional software environments and reduces task completion time by approximately 80% compared to existing agents.

title: OSExpert: Computer-Use Agents Learning Professional Skills via Exploration

abstract:
The meteoric rise and proliferation of autonomous Large Language Model (LLM)
agents promise significant capabilities across various domains. However, their
deployment is increasingly constrained by substantial computational demands,
specifically for Graphics Processing Unit (GPU) resources. This paper addresses
the critical problem of optimizing resource utilization in LLM agent systems.
We introduce COALESCE (Cost-Optimized and Secure Agent Labour Exchange via
Skill-based Competence Estimation), a novel framework designed to enable
autonomous LLM agents to dynamically outsource specific subtasks to
specialized, cost-effective third-party LLM agents. The framework integrates
mechanisms for hybrid skill representation, dynamic skill discovery, automated
task decomposition, a unified cost model comparing internal execution costs
against external outsourcing prices, simplified market-based decision-making
algorithms, and a standardized communication protocol between LLM agents.
Comprehensive validation through 239 theoretical simulations demonstrates
41.8\% cost reduction potential, while large-scale empirical validation across
240 real LLM tasks confirms 20.3\% cost reduction with proper epsilon-greedy
exploration, establishing both theoretical viability and practical
effectiveness. The emergence of proposed open standards like Google's
Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol further underscores the need for frameworks like
COALESCE that can leverage such standards for efficient agent interaction. By
facilitating a dynamic market for agent capabilities, potentially utilizing
protocols like A2A for communication, COALESCE aims to significantly reduce
operational costs, enhance system scalability, and foster the emergence of
specialized agent economies, making complex LLM agent functionalities more
accessible and economically viable.
title: COALESCE: Economic and Security Dynamics of Skill-Based Task Outsourcing  Among Team of Autonomous LLM Agents

abstract: With the remarkable advancement of AI agents, the number of their equipped tools is increasing rapidly. However, integrating all tool information into the limited model context becomes impractical, highlighting the need for efficient tool retrieval methods. In this regard, dominant methods primarily rely on semantic similarities between tool descriptions and user queries to retrieve relevant tools. However, they often consider each tool independently, overlooking dependencies between tools, which may lead to the omission of prerequisite tools for successful task execution. To deal with this defect, in this paper, we propose Tool Graph Retriever (TGR), which exploits the dependencies among tools to learn better tool representations for retrieval. First, we construct a dataset termed TDI300K to train a discriminator for identifying tool dependencies. Then, we represent all candidate tools as a tool dependency graph and use graph convolution to integrate the dependencies into their representations. Finally, these updated tool representations are employed for online retrieval. Experimental results on several commonly used datasets show that our TGR can bring a performance improvement to existing dominant methods, achieving SOTA performance. Moreover, in-depth analyses also verify the importance of tool dependencies and the effectiveness of our TGR.
title: Tool Graph Retriever: Exploring Dependency Graph-based Tool Retrieval for Large Language Models

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly rely on agentic capabilities-iterative retrieval, tool use, and decision-making-to overcome the limits of static, parametric knowledge. Yet existing agentic frameworks treat external information as unstructured text and fail to leverage the topological dependencies inherent in real-world data.
